expectations.text: Output references specific rangeops files or patterns (schema files, existing models, routes)
expectations.evidence: Scope doc references: 'permissions.ts' for waiver_template and waiver resources, 'proxy.ts' for subdomain routing, customer model fields (email, phone), Drizzle ORM schema conventions with organizationId, featureFlags JSONB in org settings. Transcript Turn 5: 'the permissions system already distinguishes between waiver_template and waiver'. Turn 9 lists 6 specific codebase observations. Feasibility Assessment section names concrete files and patterns.

expectations.text: No functional requirements slip through (no approval workflows, field-level rules, permission models, specific validation logic, or data schema designs)
expectations.evidence: The interview stays at capability level throughout. Minor signing is framed as 'one adult signs for one or more minors' (capability, not field spec). E-signature is described as 'typed name + consent affirmation + audit metadata' (approach, not schema design). No questions about specific fields, validation rules, approval workflows, or permission logic. The metrics.json confirms: 'requirements_level_questions_that_slipped_through: []' and notes 'Data retention policy was explicitly deferred to /define.' The scope doc's in/out lists describe capabilities, not implementation details.

expectations.text: All 6 domains covered: problem/motivation, stakeholders/impact, feasibility, high-level scope, risks/assumptions, recommendation
expectations.evidence: Transcript covers: problem/motivation (Turns 1-2), stakeholders/impact (Turns 3-4), high-level scope (Turns 5-8), feasibility (Turns 9-10), risks/assumptions (Turns 11-12), recommendation (Turns 13-14). All six domains are present in both the transcript and the scope doc as distinct sections. Metrics.json confirms domains_covered_count: 6.

expectations.text: Scope doc has these sections: Problem Statement, Stakeholders, Feasibility Assessment, High-Level Scope (with in/out/investigate), Key Risks & Assumptions, Recommendation with verdict, Pipeline Status
expectations.evidence: Scope doc contains all required sections: '## Problem Statement' (line 7), '## Stakeholders' (line 9), '## Feasibility Assessment' (line 15), '## High-Level Scope' with subsections 'Likely in for v1' (line 22), 'Likely out for v1' (line 31), 'Needs investigation' (line 39), '## Key Risks & Assumptions' (line 42), '## Recommendation' with 'Verdict: Go' (line 52), '## Pipeline Status' with table (line 56).

expectations.text: Clear Go / No-Go / Investigate Further verdict with rationale
expectations.evidence: Scope doc line 52: '**Verdict**: Go' followed by rationale: 'This is a launch blocker and table-stakes feature with clear operator demand. The technical foundation is already partially in place (permissions stubbed, customer model ready, tenant routing operational). The scope is well-defined and bounded. Estimated at 2-3 weeks of focused work. No major technical risks -- the primary uncertainty is legal, which is low-likelihood and can be mitigated with a review.'

Scope doc Recommendation section ends with: 'Proceed to `/define` for detailed requirements.' Transcript Turn 14: 'Review this and tell me what to change. When satisfied, run `/define`.' Metrics.json confirms define_suggested: true.

expectations.text: Explicit 'likely in for v1' and 'likely out for v1' lists
expectations.evidence: Scope doc contains '**Likely in for v1:**' (line 22) with 7 bullet items (template management, signing page, native e-signature, waiver status, expiration tracking, minor/guardian signing, public URL) and '**Likely out for v1:**' (line 31) with 6 bullet items (renewal reminders, kiosk mode, payment collection, analytics, third-party e-sign, template diffing). Additionally includes '**Needs investigation:**' (line 39) with 1 item (data retention).

claims.claim: Permissions layer already defines waiver_template and waiver resources
claims.evidence: Transcript codebase context confirms: 'Permissions layer (permissions.ts) already defines waiver_template and waiver resources with role-based CRUD across owner/admin/member roles.' This was observed from actual codebase exploration before the interview began.

claims.claim: Customer model has email and phone fields
claims.evidence: Transcript codebase context: 'Customer model exists with email, phone, emergency contact fields.'

claims.claim: No waiver-related schema, routes, or components exist yet
claims.evidence: Transcript codebase context: 'No waiver-related schema, routes, or components exist.' Also stated in scope doc: 'No schema tables, routes, or UI exist.'

claims.claim: This is the first customer-facing (non-admin) page in the platform
claims.evidence: Transcript Turn 9: 'the platform doesn't have yet is any customer-facing (non-admin) page experience. Every current route is behind the org admin layout.' Confirmed via codebase exploration.

Reviewed all 14 turns. Questions addressed: timing motivation, pain points, stakeholders, business value, v1 capabilities, signing experience, exclusions, minor scope complexity, technical constraints, e-sign approach, legal risk, other risks, sizing, feature naming. None asked for specific field definitions, validation rules, approval workflows, or permission assignments. The closest is Turn 10 discussing e-signature metadata, but this is framed as an approach decision, not a field-level spec.

claims.claim: Estimated at 2-3 weeks of focused work
claims.evidence: Transcript Turn 13: 'I'd estimate this at roughly 2-3 weeks of focused work.' User confirmed: 'That sounds about right.' Scope doc repeats: 'Estimated at 2-3 weeks of focused work.'

suggestions.assertion: Output references specific rangeops files or patterns (schema files, existing models, routes)
suggestions.reason: This passes if the output mentions even one file. A stronger version would require a minimum count or that the references are integrated into the feasibility analysis rather than just listed. The current assertion would pass if the output name-dropped 'permissions.ts' once in passing.

No assertion checks interview quality -- whether questions were open-ended, used AskUserQuestion correctly, or followed up meaningfully on user answers. The transcript shows strong follow-up (e.g., Turn 8 drilling into minor signing complexity after the user raised it in Turn 7), but this quality goes unmeasured.

No assertion checks that the scope doc accurately reflects the interview content. An output that fabricated scope items not discussed in the interview would pass all current assertions. For example, verifying that 'minor/guardian signing' appears in the in-list because the user explicitly requested it in Turn 7, not because the model invented it.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions cover structural completeness well (sections exist, lists present, verdict included) but don't verify content accuracy or interview quality. The hardest-to-fake assertion is #2 (no functional requirements slip through) which requires genuine altitude discipline. Consider adding a traceability assertion: every in-scope item should map to a specific user statement in the transcript.
